title: Implement transparent HTTPS proxy core with TLS SNI inspection
description:
Implement the core transparent HTTPS proxy that can intercept port 443 traffic, inspect TLS SNI during handshake, and route based on domain names.

📋 Governing Documentation:
- docs/DOMAIN_BASED_FORWARDING_DESIGN.md v0.1 - Sections 'Technical Implementation' and 'Architecture'

Core Components:
1. TransparentHTTPSProxy class with connection handling
2. SNI extraction from TLS Client Hello packets
3. Routing logic for navis.local vs other domains
4. pfctl rules to redirect port 443 to proxy
5. Basic connection relay between client and destinations

Technical Requirements:
- Intercept all port 443 traffic via pfctl
- Parse TLS Client Hello to extract SNI without breaking handshake
- Route navis.local to 127.0.0.1:47621
- Route other traffic to original destinations
- Maintain end-to-end encryption
- Handle concurrent connections efficiently

Acceptance Criteria:
- Proxy binds to port 8443 and receives redirected traffic
- Can extract SNI from TLS handshake correctly
- Routes navis.local to NavisAI daemon
- Routes other domains to original destinations
- Passes basic connectivity tests

Dependencies:
- This is Phase 1 of navisai-a1p
- Must integrate with existing daemon architecture

title: Performance optimization and comprehensive testing
description:
Optimize the transparent proxy system for production performance and create comprehensive test coverage to ensure reliability and minimal overhead.

📋 Governing Documentation:
- docs/DOMAIN_BASED_FORWARDING_DESIGN.md v0.1 - Sections 'Performance Considerations' and 'Testing Strategy'

Performance Optimizations:
1. Connection pooling for frequently accessed destinations
2. DNS resolution caching with intelligent TTL
3. Zero-copy data relay using Node.js streams
4. Certificate caching with memory-efficient storage
5. Selective TLS interception (skip HTTP/1.1 upgrades)
6. CPU and memory usage optimization

Target Benchmarks:
- Latency overhead: < 5ms per connection
- Throughput: > 500 Mbps sustained
- Memory usage: < 50MB at idle
- CPU usage: < 2% when idle, < 10% under load
- Concurrent connections: Support 1000+ active

Test Coverage Requirements:
1. Unit tests for all core components (90%+ coverage)
2. Integration tests for end-to-end proxy flow
3. Performance tests with realistic workloads
4. Security tests for certificate handling
5. Compatibility tests across macOS versions
6. Stress tests for connection limits

Test Categories:
- SNI extraction accuracy
- Routing logic verification
- Certificate generation and validation
- Connection pooling efficiency
- Memory leak detection
- Concurrent connection handling
- Error recovery scenarios
- Security boundary validation

Monitoring Integration:
- Prometheus metrics for performance tracking
- Health check endpoints for monitoring
- Structured logging for troubleshooting
- Performance profiling hooks
- Alert thresholds for critical metrics

Acceptance Criteria:
- Meets all performance benchmarks
- Passes 90%+ test coverage
- Handles 1000+ concurrent connections
- No memory leaks after 24h stress test
- Graceful degradation under high load
- Comprehensive documentation for troubleshooting

Dependencies:
- Requires all previous phases to be complete
- Final integration task for navisai-a1p

title: macOS bridge uses wrong pf anchor/rules (navis vs navisai/*)
description:
Found a major mismatch: apps/daemon/src/transparent-proxy.js installs pf rules into anchors navisai/proxy + navisai/filter, but apps/daemon/src/bridge.js setupMacOS() installs a broad rdr rule into anchor 'navis' (not navisai/*) and redirects all :443 (from any to any) to 8443. This contradicts the coexistence contract and can break dev tools.

Fix:
- Bridge should not install its own broad pfConf; it should delegate to TransparentHTTPSProxy.createPfRules() only, or install matching scoped rules into navisai/proxy + navisai/filter.
- Remove/rename anchorName='navis' and ensure /etc/pf.conf anchor points match.

📋 Governing Documentation:
- docs/NETWORKING.md (coexistence contract)
- docs/DOMAIN_BASED_FORWARDING_DESIGN.md
- docs/SECURITY.md

- Methods have incorrect indentation (2 spaces instead of 4)
- Class structure broken after initializeServices() method

- Verify syntax with: node -c apps/daemon/daemon.js

title: Add dynamic dev server detection and auto-mapping

- app.localhost → First detected app server

- Preserve manual mappings in configuration

- Generates valid certificates for mapped domains
- Updates mappings when servers start/stop

title: Isolate navis.local from dev tools (ServBay/nginx) via dedicated IP alias
description:
ServBay/nginx and other dev tools must never be impacted by Navis bridge.

Observed:
- Requests for https://navis.local can land in ServBay nginx default vhost (logs show server: _ attempting to serve /welcome).
- Root cause: current macOS pf approach relies on redirecting :443 on the primary LAN IP and SNI-routing, which still intercepts local traffic and is subject to pf localhost limitations (navisai-5zu).

Goal:
- True encapsulation: only navis.local traffic should be redirected; all other domains on :443 must be untouched at the socket layer.

Proposed direction:
- Allocate a dedicated local IP alias for navis.local (secondary address on the active interface).
- Advertise that IP via mDNS A record for navis.local.
- pf rdr only for traffic destined to that dedicated IP (not the primary IP), eliminating interference with dev tools bound to 0.0.0.0:443.

📋 Governing Documentation:
- docs/NETWORKING.md (canonical origin + bridge model)
- docs/DOMAIN_BASED_FORWARDING_DESIGN.md (coexistence contract)
- docs/MACOS_SETUP_EXPERIENCE.md (Apple-like UX on device)

title: Implement passthrough routing for non-navis.local SNI
description:
Transparent proxy currently intercepts all :443 traffic. To preserve dev-tool coexistence (ServBay, etc.), implement strict routing:
- If SNI == navis.local (and any explicitly allowed Navis domains): terminate TLS and route to Navis daemon.
- Else: TCP passthrough to original destination (no TLS termination, no cert minting), preserving the peer certificate of the target service.
- If SNI missing/unknown: safe fallback should NOT MITM; prefer passthrough or fail-closed with clear log.

title: Implement certificate management for multiple domains
description:
Create a comprehensive certificate management system that can generate, store, and serve certificates for multiple domains during TLS interception.

📋 Governing Documentation:
- docs/DOMAIN_BASED_FORWARDING_DESIGN.md v0.1 - Section 'Certificate Management'
- docs/SECURITY.md v0.2 - Security requirements for TLS handling

Core Requirements:
1. Generate unique root CA per NavisAI installation
2. On-demand certificate generation for any domain
3. Secure storage of CA private key in system Keychain
4. Certificate caching with appropriate TTL
5. Automatic CA trust installation with user consent
6. Certificate validation for destination connections

Implementation Details:
- Use Node.js crypto module for certificate generation
- Implement X.509 certificate parsing and validation
- Support wildcard certificates (*.localhost)
- Implement Certificate Revocation List (CRL) support
- Provide CLI commands for certificate management

Security Measures:
- CA private key encrypted at rest
- Limited certificate validity (90 days default)
- Secure random number generation
- Certificate pinning for navis.local
- Clear audit trail of all certificate operations

CLI Integration:
- ./navisai setup --install-ca
- ./navisai certs list
- ./navisai certs generate <domain>
- ./navisai certs revoke <domain>
- ./navisai ca trust --remove

Acceptance Criteria:
- Generates valid certificates accepted by browsers
- Securely stores CA private key
- Auto-trusts CA in system Keychain (macOS)
- Caches certificates efficiently
- Validates destination certificates
- Provides clear error messages for certificate issues

Dependencies:
- Requires navisai-a1p-a (proxy core) to use certificates
- Requires navisai-a1p-b (dev detection) for domain mappings

title: Make Beads DB selection deterministic (multi-DB + global socket warnings)
description:
Issues chained together like beads. A lightweight issue tracker with first-class dependency support.

Usage:
  bd [command]

Available Commands:
  blocked          Show blocked issues
  close            Close one or more issues
  comment          Add a comment to an issue (alias for 'comments add')
  comments         View or manage comments on an issue
  compact          Compact old closed issues to save space
  completion       Generate the autocompletion script for the specified shell
  config           Manage configuration settings
  create           Create a new issue (or multiple issues from markdown file)
  daemon           Run background sync daemon
  daemons          Manage multiple bd daemons
  delete           Delete one or more issues and clean up references
  dep              Manage dependencies
  detect-pollution Detect test issues that leaked into production database
  doctor           Check beads installation health
  duplicates       Find and optionally merge duplicate issues
  edit             Edit an issue field in $EDITOR
  epic             Epic management commands
  export           Export issues to JSONL format
  help             Help about any command
  import           Import issues from JSONL format
  info             Show database and daemon information
  init             Initialize bd in the current directory
  label            Manage issue labels
  list             List issues
  merge            Merge duplicate issues into a single issue
  migrate          Migrate database to current version
  migrate-hash-ids Migrate sequential IDs to hash-based IDs
  onboard          Display instructions for configuring AGENTS.md
  quickstart       Quick start guide for bd
  ready            Show ready work (no blockers, open or in-progress)
  rename-prefix    Rename the issue prefix for all issues
  reopen           Reopen one or more closed issues
  repair-deps      Find and fix orphaned dependency references
  repo             Manage multiple repository configuration
  restore          Restore full history of a compacted issue from git
  show             Show issue details
  stale            Show stale issues (not updated recently)
  stats            Show statistics
  status           Show issue database overview
  sync             Synchronize issues with git remote
  template         Manage issue templates
  update           Update one or more issues
  validate         Run comprehensive database health checks
  version          Print version information

Flags:
      --actor string     Actor name for audit trail (default: $BD_ACTOR or $USER)
      --db string        Database path (default: auto-discover .beads/*.db)
  -h, --help             help for bd
      --json             Output in JSON format
      --no-auto-flush    Disable automatic JSONL sync after CRUD operations
      --no-auto-import   Disable automatic JSONL import when newer than DB
      --no-daemon        Force direct storage mode, bypass daemon if running
      --no-db            Use no-db mode: load from JSONL, no SQLite
      --sandbox          Sandbox mode: disables daemon and auto-sync

Use "bd [command] --help" for more information about a command. emits warnings about old global daemon socket and multiple beads DBs in the directory hierarchy. This can cause confusion and non-deterministic automation.

Tasks:
- Decide repo policy (e.g., require running local project daemon, avoid global ~/.beads).
- Update scripts to pass  explicitly where needed.
- Add doctor/check to detect pollution and give remediation.

- docs/BEADS_AGENT_GUIDE.md

title: daemon-ws: Add log events over WebSocket
description:
To support real-time log streaming in the CLI, implement log events that are sent over the WebSocket connection.

**Requirements:**
1. Add 'log' event type to WebSocket message types
2. Stream log entries as they are written to file/console
3. Include log level, timestamp, and message
4. Buffer logs for WebSocket clients that connect late
5. Allow filtering by log level via query params
6. Rate limit to prevent flooding

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Log events are emitted over WebSocket
- [ ] CLI receives real-time log updates
- [ ] Each log entry includes level, timestamp, message
- [ ] Recent logs are buffered for new connections
- [ ] Log level filtering works (e.g., ?level=error)
- [ ] No performance impact on daemon operation
